response: The differential equation provided combines logistic growth with a time-dependent sinusoidal forcing term, creating a non-autonomous system that generally lacks a simple closed-form analytical solution in terms of elementary functions. To address the first part, we rearrange the equation as $\frac{dP}{dt} = P \left[ r \left(1 - \frac{P}{K}\right) + A \sin\left(\frac{2\pi t}{12}\right) \right]$, which can be rewritten as $\frac{dP}{dt} = P \left[ \left(r + A \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{6}\right)\right) - \frac{r}{K}P \right]$. This structure resembles a Bernoulli differential equation or a time-varying logistic equation where the effective growth rate $r_{eff}(t) = r + A \sin(\frac{\pi t}{6})$ fluctuates monthly. Substituting the given values $r=0.3$, $K=1000$, and $A=0.1$, the equation becomes $\frac{dP}{dt} = P \left[ 0.3 + 0.1 \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{6}\right) - \frac{0.3}{1000}P \right]$. While an exact general solution $P(t)$ involving $P_0$ requires solving the integral of the time-varying coefficient, the solution can be expressed implicitly or via a series expansion. The standard approach involves the substitution $u = 1/P$, transforming the equation into a linear first-order differential equation: $\frac{du}{dt} + u \left( 0.3 + 0.1 \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{6}\right) \right) = -\frac{0.3}{1000}$. Solving this linear equation yields $u(t) = e^{-\int (0.3 + 0.1 \sin(\frac{\pi t}{6})) dt} \left[ \int -\frac{0.3}{1000} e^{\int (0.3 + 0.1 \sin(\frac{\pi t}{6})) dt} dt + C \right]$.
